questions.text: Who popularized the term 'Biodiversity'?
questions.explanation: Edward Wilson, a sociobiologist, used it to describe combined diversity at all levels of biological organization.

questions.text: Diversity at the species level is called:
questions.explanation: Example: Western Ghats have a greater amphibian species diversity than Eastern Ghats.

questions.text: India has more than 50,000 genetically different strains of rice. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Genetic diversity refers to variations within a single species.

questions.text: According to IUCN (2004), the total number of plant and animal species described so far is slightly more than:
questions.explanation: Robert May's global estimate, however, places the number at about 7 million.

questions.text: More than 70% of all the species recorded are:
questions.explanation: Among animals, insects are the most species-rich taxonomic group (70%).

questions.text: India has only 2.4% of the world's land area, but its share of global species diversity is:
questions.explanation: This makes India one of the 12 mega-diversity countries of the world.

questions.text: Biodiversity increases as we move:
questions.explanation: Tropical regions (near equator) harbor more species than temperate or polar regions.

questions.text: The Species-Area relationship was given by:
questions.explanation: He observed that within a region, species richness increased with increasing explored area, but only up to a limit.

questions.text: The 'Rivet Popper Hypothesis' was proposed by:
questions.explanation: He used the analogy of an airplane to explain the importance of every species in an ecosystem.

questions.text: The 'Evil Quartet' refers to:
options: Four major causes of biodiversity loss
questions.explanation: Habitat loss/fragmentation, Over-exploitation, Alien species invasion, and Co-extinctions.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most important cause driving animals and plants to extinction?
questions.explanation: Tropical rain forests once covering 14% of land now cover less than 6%.

questions.text: The Nile Perch introduced into Lake Victoria led to the extinction of:
questions.explanation: More than 200 species of cichlid fish became extinct due to this alien species invasion.

questions.text: Steller's sea cow and Passenger pigeon became extinct due to:
questions.explanation: When 'need' turns to 'greed', it leads to over-exploitation.

questions.text: Lantana, Parthenium, and Eichhornia are examples of:
questions.explanation: These species cause environmental damage and threaten native species.

questions.text: In-situ conservation refers to:
questions.explanation: Protecting the whole ecosystem to save a species (e.g., National Parks).

questions.text: How many 'Biodiversity Hotspots' are identified in the world?
questions.explanation: Initially 25, now total 34 hotspots are identified for high endemism and accelerated habitat loss.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an In-situ conservation method?
questions.explanation: Zoological parks are Ex-situ (off-site) conservation methods.

questions.text: Sacred groves are found in:
questions.explanation: These are forest patches protected by communities for religious reasons.
Sacred groves are often the last refuges for many rare plants.

questions.text: The 'Earth Summit' (1992) was held in:
questions.explanation: It was held for the conservation of biodiversity and sustainable utilization of its benefits.

questions.text: Cryopreservation of gametes is a part of:
questions.explanation: It involves preserving threatened species' gametes in viable conditions at low temperatures.

questions.text: The 'World Summit' on Sustainable Development (2002) was held in:
questions.explanation: 190 countries pledged to reduce the rate of biodiversity loss by 2010.

questions.text: Species restricted to a specific region and not found anywhere else are:
questions.explanation: Biodiversity hotspots have high levels of species richness and endemism.

questions.text: The graph of log S = log C + Z log A is a:
questions.explanation: On a logarithmic scale, the species-area relationship is a straight line.

questions.text: Value of regression coefficient 'Z' for frugivorous birds in tropical forests is:
questions.explanation: In very large areas like entire continents, the slope (Z) is much steeper.

questions.text: Which of the following is an argument for 'Narrowly Utilitarian' biodiversity conservation?
questions.explanation: Narrowly utilitarian reasons focus on direct economic benefits like food, fiber, and medicine.

questions.text: What percentage of drugs currently sold in the market are derived from plants?
questions.explanation: Plants contribute significantly to modern medicine.

questions.text: Exploring molecular, genetic, and species-level diversity for products of economic importance is:
questions.explanation: Bioprospecting helps in discovering new medicines or resources.

questions.text: The Amazon rain forest is often called:
questions.explanation: It is estimated to produce 20% of the total oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.

questions.text: When a host fish species becomes extinct, its unique assemblage of parasites also meets the same fate. This is:
questions.explanation: This occurs when two species are obligatorily associated.

questions.text: The recent extinction of Dodo occurred in:
questions.explanation: The Dodo (Mauritius), Quagga (Africa), and Thylacine (Australia) are examples of recent extinctions.

questions.text: Which group of animals is most vulnerable to extinction?
questions.explanation: Globally, 32% of all amphibian species face the threat of extinction.

questions.text: Biodiversity 'hotspots' cover less than ________ of the Earth's land area.
questions.explanation: Despite the small area, they harbor a massive number of species.

questions.text: One of the Ex-situ conservation methods for plants is:
questions.explanation: Seed banks store seeds of different genetic strains of commercially important plants for long periods.

questions.text: The historical convention on Biological Diversity held in 1992 was called:
questions.explanation: Held in Rio de Janeiro, it was a turning point for global biodiversity awareness.

questions.text: How many National Parks are there in India? (Approx)
questions.explanation: As per NCERT, India has 90 National Parks and 448 Wildlife Sanctuaries.

questions.text: The Western Ghats of India is a:
questions.explanation: The Western Ghats-Sri Lanka region is one of the world's biodiversity hotspots.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a reason for tropical biodiversity richness?
questions.explanation: Unlike temperate regions, tropics have been free from glaciations for millions of years.

questions.text: Genetic diversity of 'Rauwolfia vomitoria' relates to the concentration of:
questions.explanation: The medicinal plant shows variations in the potency and concentration of reserpine.

questions.text: Introduction of Clarias gariepinus (African catfish) for aquaculture poses a threat to:
questions.explanation: It is an illegal alien species that competes with local catfish.

questions.text: The ethical argument for conservation says:
questions.explanation: Every species has an intrinsic right to exist; we have a moral duty to care for them.
